reasoning: As a conservative prescriber with only 4 distinct diabetes molecules and a low branded-share footprint (14.3%), I'm not going to jump aggressively onto a newly launched agent. I'd want to see real-world tolerability, formulary placement under Part D, and cost/coverage for my Medicare patients before committing broadly. A selective pilot in a few well-suited patients—perhaps those inadequately controlled on current therapy—is the natural starting point for someone with my adoption archetype and zero Eli Lilly relationship.

Even with a conservative stance, the GLP-1 class momentum in 2022 is unmistakable. My current GLP-1 share is only 14.4% of diabetes claims, well below where guidelines and cardiovascular-outcomes data would support. With tirzepatide adding another option and semaglutide already shifting expectations around efficacy, I'd expect gradual but meaningful uptake—partly from new starts and partly from switching patients off less effective agents. The trajectory is clearly upward.

My branded share is already quite low at 14.3%, reflecting a strong generic preference typical of a conservative PCP. The question specifically asks about oral agents, and tirzepatide is injectable, so it won't directly shift my oral branded/generic mix. New branded orals aren't entering the market in a way that would pull me away from metformin, generic SGLT2 inhibitors, or other established generics. I expect the mix to stay about the same.

Diabetes already comprises 22.8% of my Part D claims—a substantial share for a family physician. My panel of 84 beneficiaries is relatively stable, and I don't anticipate a sudden influx of new diabetic patients or a change in my practice mix that would meaningfully alter this proportion. Aging of existing patients may nudge diabetes prevalence up slightly, but not enough to call it a meaningful increase over the coming months.

With 518 claims across 84 beneficiaries, my Part D practice is modest and stable. I'm not expanding aggressively, nor am I winding down. No major practice changes are on the horizon that would shift overall Part D volume meaningfully in either direction. I expect roughly similar scale going forward.

I currently touch only 4 distinct diabetes molecules—a narrow formulary reflective of my conservative approach. The arrival of tirzepatide gives me a clinically distinct option (dual GIP/GLP-1 mechanism) that I'd likely add for selective use, pushing me to 5 molecules. Even if I'm cautious, the availability of a new class-adjacent agent with a different mechanism naturally widens the toolkit I'd consider for appropriate patients.

As a conservative PCP with only 5 distinct diabetes molecules and 14% GLP-1 share, I'm not going to jump in aggressively on a brand-new agent. I'll pilot it in a few patients who are failing current therapies—maybe those already on a GLP-1 who need better efficacy—before expanding. Formulary and prior auth uncertainties for Medicare patients also argue for a cautious start.

Even starting from a modest 14% GLP-1 share, the class momentum is strong. Guidelines increasingly favor GLP-1s for cardiovascular benefit, and tirzepatide's arrival adds another option that may pull in patients who weren't ideal candidates for existing agents. I expect my GLP-1 share to climb meaningfully, though from a conservative base it won't be dramatic in absolute terms.

My branded share is only 24.3%, reflecting a generic-leaning posture. But the GLP-1 class is almost entirely branded, and if I'm prescribing more GLP-1s, that inevitably pushes the branded share upward. I won't abandon generics for oral agents, but the class shift toward injectable branded therapies will tilt the mix.

Diabetes is 16.3% of my claims—stable and in line with a typical family medicine panel. I don't see a reason for it to spike or drop meaningfully. My patient population isn't changing dramatically, and while I may treat diabetes more intensively with newer agents, the share of total claims devoted to diabetes should stay about the same.

With roughly 1,000 claims across 167 beneficiaries, this is a stable Part D practice. As a conservative PCP I'm not dramatically expanding or contracting my Medicare panel. Some churn is normal, but the overall volume should remain roughly similar.

I currently touch 5 distinct diabetes molecules—a narrow footprint consistent with a conservative approach. Adding tirzepatide selectively might bring it to 6, but in practice I'd likely be substituting it for another GLP-1 in some patients rather than layering on entirely new mechanisms. Net breadth change is minimal; I'm not suddenly reaching for SGLT2s or other classes I haven't been using.

As a conservative prescriber with low pharma engagement and a modest 13.6% GLP-1 share, I'm cautious with new agents. Tirzepatide looks promising but I'll want to see real-world data, formulary positioning, and patient tolerance before broad adoption. Selective use in patients failing current therapy is the most realistic approach for my practice pattern.

GLP-1 momentum is building with strong outcomes data, and with tirzepatide entering the space, I expect my GLP-1 share to climb from its current 13.6%. Even as a conservative prescriber, the evidence base and patient interest are pushing in this direction, and having more options makes me more comfortable prescribing the class.

The question specifies oral agents. My oral diabetes prescribing is already heavily generic—metformin, sulfonylureas, pioglitazone. GLP-1s and tirzepatide are injectables, so they don't shift the oral branded/generic calculus. I expect my oral branded share to stay around its current level.

My diabetes share at 18.9% reflects a stable internal medicine panel. I'm not specializing or marketing myself as a diabetes-focused practice, so I don't expect the proportion of diabetes-related prescribing to shift meaningfully relative to my other chronic disease management.

As a conservative adopter with only 13.6% GLP-1 share and no pharma engagement pushing me toward new brands, I'm not going to jump in aggressively. As a conservative prescriber with only 11.3% GLP-1 share of my diabetes claims and just 6 distinct diabetes molecules on my panel, I'm not going to jump aggressively onto tirzepatide. Formulary uncertainty for a brand-new agent under Medicare Part D is real, and with my low pharma engagement I'm not being pushed toward early adoption. Selective use is the realistic posture.

My GLP-1 share is only 11.3% of diabetes claims—well below what guidelines would suggest for a population likely carrying cardiovascular and renal risk. But as a conservative prescriber, my adoption curve is slow. The class momentum is undeniable, and I may nudge a few more patients onto GLP-1s, but 'meaningfully' higher share requires overcoming cost barriers, prior authorizations, and my own cautious prescribing habits. I expect a modest uptick at best, effectively flat over the coming months.

I don't see a meaningful shift in either direction.

My GLP-1 share is already low at 9.4%, and as a conservative PCP I tend to stick with established patterns. While the class has momentum, my patient population and prescribing habits change slowly. I currently touch 6 distinct diabetes molecules—a limited but focused toolkit typical of a conservative PCP. As an early adopter with 41% GLP-1 share and high branded prescribing, I'm inclined to try tirzepatide—but Medicare Part D formulary positioning for a newly launched branded injectable is uncertain mid-2022. Active across-the-board use would be premature given coverage and cost barriers my Medicare patients will face.

I'm already at 41% GLP-1 share—well above average—and the class momentum is unmistakable. Tirzepatide adds another compelling option, and my early-adopter tendency means I'll reach for GLP-1s even more readily now that I have a dual agonist with impressive A1c and weight data. Each new agent also makes it easier to find a product each patient tolerates, reducing discontinuation. I expect meaningful share growth.

Diabetes is 17.8% of my Part D claims—a stable, established share for an internist in LA. New drugs don't create new diabetists; they change how I treat existing patients.
